category.name: zoo.collectableItems
category.item: If no one takes care of a "transitional meadow" for a long time, a wild forest will soon grow on it again.
In spring, the meadows turn into a sea of ​​wildflowers. And your animals love them!
A transitional meadow is created by clearing trees. Once the forest is gone, small grass shoots will first grow there.
Mushrooms are tasty mushrooms and grow, for example, in North America.
The meadows are also often home to elves, who emerge from their hiding places at night. These shy but friendly creatures often share their homes with hares.
The sparkling icicles form in freezing temperatures. Of course, the warmth of the sun melts them again.
Few people know this, but penguins also like popsicles and sometimes steal them from tourists.
Ice cubes are the perfect way to cool down a hot drink. They provide refreshment on a hot summer day.
Ice skating is an amazing sport. Historians believe that it was invented about 4,000 years ago. The Finns probably contributed to the invention.
Igloo means house to the Eskimos, who are the original inhabitants of North America.
People have been making wings from bamboo for a long time. The lightweight yet strong material allows them to reach incredible heights.
Bamboo is a collective name for several genera of woody grasses.
The gong is a nice decoration for your bamboo garden. And that exotic sound... seems to be made to start meditation.
An Asian-style statue will add an atmosphere of calm and peace to the bamboo garden.
Hang a pretty lantern in your bamboo garden and brighten the night with a soft light.
Parrots like to eat seeds and nuts, but they will also enjoy fruit. It doesn't matter if it's fresh or dried, the main thing is that it's sweet.
The jungles of the Amazon region of Peru are home to many parrots, and the rainforest provides them with an ideal habitat.
Parrots actually have 4 claws. Two are in front and two in back. That way they won't fall off the branch even if they fall asleep.
Parrots are very social and popular pets, but they need plenty of space to run around.
Parrots are actually pirates who were transformed into colorful birds by evil magicians.
Hares are vegetarians. In addition to grains, they eat grass, herbs, lettuce, and colorful flowers.
European wild hares originally lived in Portugal, Spain and northwest Africa, but they have also reached Australia.
Rabbit's feet are a symbol of good luck in many cultures.
Hares have been around for centuries. Fossils show that hare-like creatures lived in China 60 million years ago.
Once a year, the Easter Bunny arrives and hides chocolate and colored eggs.
Penguins are absolute gourmets. They prefer fish, but they also enjoy sea creatures.
The Subpolar Islands are home to one of the largest and heaviest penguins - the emperor penguin.
A penguin's walk on land looks clumsy, but in the water, the penguin is an agile swimmer and diver.
Penguins are very social and competitive animals that often enjoy dancing underwater.
Some people also call a tuxedo a penguin tailcoat because it strikingly resembles the appearance of a penguin.
Most turtles are omnivores, but some species specialize in only one type of food, such as jellyfish.
Elephant tortoises come from the Galapagos Islands, which belong to Ecuador in South America.
The feet of a water turtle have webbed feet between the toes, while those of land turtles have feet that are more like stumps.
Baby giant tortoises communicate with their siblings while still in the egg. They simply bump into each other with their egg shells.
Elephant tortoises can live to be over 100 years old, but they never grow a gray beard.
Lions, like all felines, are carnivores. They feed on wildebeest, zebras, and buffalo.
Today they live mainly in national parks such as in Tanzania or on the coast of East Africa.
They command respect with their large paws and their claws firmly grip their prey.
White lions are actually completely normal lions, only their fur has been bleached by the sun.
Although they inhabit savannas and steppes, they are often referred to as the "kings of the jungle".
Peacocks eat mostly grain and grains, but from time to time they will also eat flowers and the occasional beetle.
In India, the peacock has been the national bird since 1963. Indians value these birds because they hunt poisonous cobras.
Male peacocks are quite vain. They can spend hours combing their beautiful feathers with their claws. And finally they spread them out in all their glory.
Peacock tail feathers are beautiful. In Greek mythology, peacock eyes are a symbol of the guardian Argus, who guarded Zeus's lover Io.
Peacocks can do some pretty cool stunts to arouse the interest of females, and females also like to flirt with them.
Pandas prefer to chew bamboo, especially the leaves and young twigs. Because they are large animals, they consume a lot of it.
The last wild pandas live in China, the largest country in the world. Unfortunately, there are only a few of these giant pandas left.
Giant pandas have a crooked thumb. They use it to hold food.
Baby pandas are very small indeed. They weigh only 100 to 200 grams and are about 15-17 cm long.
Clever pandas use bamboo sticks to skewer their food, just as people eat with chopsticks in Asia.
Sonia is a master of cuddling. Hire her and you'll get 12% more BZ for her services!
The bear cubs would just cuddle all the time. Some of them don't want to leave their caregiver at all.
With her cheerfully spinning top, Soňa can cheer up even the grumpiest rhino.
Cute house cats like to chase toy mice, but lions prefer larger prey.
Monkeys are cheerful companions. They like to play with, for example, brightly colored wooden elephants.
Water is essential for all living things. The human body alone contains over 60% water.
Sailboats are relatively small boats powered by wind.
If you look around in the water with diving goggles, everything will look a little bigger. This is due to the refraction of light.
Fish often mistake lifebuoys for donuts, so sometimes they bite into the rings a little.
During the larval stage, these amphibians still have a tail, but it later disappears.
Animals that live in the snow enclosure have strikingly white fur.
Snowmen are best built from fresh snow; powder snow is too soft.
A sled can withstand a lot if it is lubricated with good wax.
The hat with a pompom hides a small bell that can be used to attract birds to feed them.
When the temperature is below freezing, water vapor forms snowflakes that resemble wads of cotton wool.
Some plants need moist, mineral-rich soil to grow, which is ideal in swamps.
Water lily seeds spend 2 to 3 days on the surface before sinking to the bottom and starting to germinate.
There are over 2,000 species of fireflies in the world. Most of them use light signals to find each other.
The common flycatcher doesn't just like to catch tasty flies. It also treats itself to chewing gum.
The canoe was probably invented 6,000 years ago.
Adult flamingos are pink because they absorb special pigments called carotenoids with their food.
The United States is home to 3 different species of flamingos.
Experts believe that flamingos stand on one leg to keep warm, as they spend a significant portion of the day in cold water.
Flamingos are very social birds, often living in flocks of thousands.
If you feed flamingos colorful jelly candies, their feathers will become more colorful.
Kangaroos are marsupials and carry their young in a pouch at all times after birth.
The Southern Cross is featured on the Australian flag.
Trackers can tell the color of a kangaroo's paw print by its color.
The plush kangaroo is quite ticklish. Just squeeze its belly and it will immediately start laughing.
A boxing kangaroo is a common motif and points to the animals' natural defensive reflex.
The lynx is the largest feline in Europe and, in addition to small animals, can also attack deer.
Russian does not use the Latin alphabet for writing, but the Cyrillic alphabet (or another name for the Cyrillic alphabet).
The lynx has a characteristic track in the snow - it lacks claw marks.
Lynx cubs are breastfed until they are 5 months old.
If a lamp is shone on a lynx, it immediately changes color.
Chimpanzees are omnivores and like fruits and nuts.
They feel comfortable in the tropical rainforest in the Democratic Republic of Congo, just like elephants, lions and hyenas.
Chimpanzees are as good with their feet as they are with their hands. Their big toe is almost as big as their thumb.
Chimpanzees live in large groups that are divided into subgroups. They include both solitary and social animals.
Scientists agree that bananas are crooked because no one bent them in a straight line in the rainforest.
Giraffes live in the African savannahs. They can go for weeks without drinking.
The Kenyan flag has 4 symbols. In the center is a Maasai shield with a spear.
The giraffe is an even-toed ungulate, as can be seen from its cloven hoof.
How cute! The little plush giraffe has two cheerful horns, just like its real counterpart.
Giraffes like to lick the blue paint off their balls, which is why this one has white spots.
The Indian elephant is smaller than the African one. It has smaller ears, just look!
The star on the flag of Malaysia has 14 points, each symbolizing 1 federal state.
Elephants have huge feet and can even dance on their toes.
Big, strong elephants were once used in war or to carry cargo. This one is just for cuddling.
Peanuts come in different variations, such as salted or roasted.
Pigeons are found all over the world. They like to eat whatever they can find.
In the land of the Maya and Aztecs, you can find many temples, pyramids, and impressive buildings.
The pigeon claw allows for comfortable seating on branches and cables. 3 claws protrude forward and one backward.
There are many types of pigeons. Over 40 different genera and over 300 species.
This ice olive is truly special. It grows in Antarctica and melts at -10°C.
If koalas can't find eucalyptus on land, they go looking for it underwater.
Australia is home to many animals and marsupials such as the koala, kangaroo, wombat, Tasmanian devil and opossum.
To climb trees, koalas simply dig their claws into the bark and swing up using their limbs.
Koala bears are popular for their cuteness. Their noses and ears give them the appearance of teddy bears.
Koalas prefer eucalyptus leaves. Of the 500 known species of eucalyptus, they only eat 70.
Slávek keeps all the enclosures clean and has a trick for it: if an animal doesn't move from a spot with a stain, he simply lathers it up!
Slávek uses a brush to clean the floor, but sometimes he also runs over animal hair.
Sławek moves his fingers happily in his gloves. Most animals can even count to five.
Thanks to the red cap, Slavek got rid of the occasional parrot visitors in his hair.
The natural sponge foams very nicely. It grows in the deep sea, where the sea is cleanest and clearest.
A witch's hat serves a vital purpose. If a witch is flying on her broomstick in a thunderstorm, it serves as a lightning rod.
This custom originates from medieval Ireland and England, when the poor would go door to door on Halloween, begging for something to eat.
One of the easiest Halloween costumes: Take a sheet, cut two holes for eyes, throw it over yourself. And we have a spooky ghost!
Want to go after zombies? Then put this horror arrow on your head. It looks like a real bullet through the head!
Sneaking through the night like Count Dracula? These terrifying vampire fangs will make everyone run away. According to Bram Stoker's 1897 novel, vampires avoid garlic and sunlight!
Best to plant immediately: It takes up to 12 years for a small fir seed to grow into a mature tree.
Warning – risk of burns! A candle flame can reach up to 1400°C at its hottest point! Ouch!

Even today, reindeer are replacing cars in many Scandinavian regions. In the darkness of the polar winter, many people hang light chains on their reindeer's antlers to avoid car accidents. Even Santa Claus thinks that's clever!
Luminous Pointer: According to Christians, on Christmas night, a bright star showed the 3 holy kings the way to Bethlehem, where Baby Jesus had just been born.
Christmas is considered the “Holiday of Love.” Perhaps especially many of these loving apples are eaten at this time.
Due to the poor vegetation, reindeer are dependent on lichens, mosses and mushrooms in winter. Enjoy your meal!
A good quarter of Finland's land area lies beyond the Arctic Circle. At the northernmost point of Finland, the sun does not set for at least 70 days in the summer.
In the spring, many smaller groups of reindeer join together and embark on a long journey in one large group, up to 5,000 kilometers long.
With their large, shovel-shaped antlers, reindeer can easily clear snow from wind-logged roads, which is why they have been valued as practical draft animals since ancient times.
It has been proven that mostly herbivorous reindeer will occasionally eat rodents, fish, birds and eggs. Something for the insatiable!
Green, green and more green, that's all I like! Nothing tastes better to zebras than fresh, juicy meadow flowers and crunchy tufts of grass.

Every year, herds of zebras make the long journey from their homeland in southern Africa to Scandinavia, where they find protection and a bit of coolness, well camouflaged in the undergrowth of the snowy forest.
Their black and white striping makes them virtually invisible to certain species of flies that transmit sleeping sickness.
A very important horse: Some herds have zebra stallions as bodyguards who keep an eye on the zebra ladies as they wander.

Birds are responsible for taking care of the zebra's fur. The little caretakers make themselves comfortable on the zebras' backs and pick out all sorts of annoying insects from their fur. Massages included!
Love passes through...the nose! Besides sweet pralines and fragrant perfumes, flowers are most often given on Valentine's Day.
The Swiss are real sweet tooths. On average, they eat the most chocolate.
Facial strengthening: Up to 34 facial muscles are involved in a kiss. Just make sure it doesn't turn into muscle fever!
In ancient Rome, the wedding ring was worn on the left hand because people believed that a vein led directly to the heart from here.
The most faithful of all animals are swans - there are known cases when a swan fell in love with a water strider.
Hippos eat only plants. They certainly don't get fat from such a diet! But they do secretly go out at night to snack on chocolate!
Hippos used to live on the Nile River, but today they are only found north of the Sahara.
You might not believe it, but hippos are excellent swimmers! They can thank the webbed feet between their toes for this, among other things.
Mom, Mom!! Hippo mothers have a very close relationship with their babies, which usually lasts their entire lives.
At birth, a baby hippo can weigh up to 55 kg. They are quite the lads!
Shining crown of gold and jewel
A frog with a crown. How cute!
Despite their intimidating appearance, rhinos are peaceful animals and prefer to eat plants.
Rhinos have incredibly strong legs, which they use to support the heavy weight of their bodies. Yet this animal is not slow at all!
While male rhinos prefer solitude, females prefer a herd where they can closely watch over their young.
Outside of Asia, rhinos are mainly found in Africa. The endless savannah provides rhinos with plenty of food.
Because of their thick skin, rhinos have trouble getting rid of vermin. Luckily, a red-headed heron will help and drive out all the annoying pests!
Crocodiles are definitely not picky when it comes to meat for dinner.
Alligators have webbed feet, which is one of the reasons they are such great swimmers!
It's hard to believe that crocodile mothers are so caring and attentive!
Alligators have everything they need in the swamps and lakes of North America. Their favorite state, of course, is Florida!
Alligator wrestling is a sport in the US. But they have to be careful not to lose their hat... or their head!
A red stone engraved with a mysterious bird.
Sunscreen is essential to survive in the Australian sun!
Catch a big wave on a surfboard.
Lemurs like Lori like to be clean. Their special cleaning claw doesn't help them clean their enclosure, but it does help keep their thick fur clean and soft.
Big-eyed monkeys are at home in the rainforest. Lori can hide perfectly in the dense foliage of the trees. She just hangs around for hours, enjoying the day.
Lori has a very strong thumb and index finger. Her firm grip is almost impossible to open. That's how she can hang the clock in one place.
Be careful, it's poisonous! The slow loris is one of the species you shouldn't pet too much. It has a poisonous gland on its shoulder that protects it from enemy bites.
Lories search for food at night. Their large eyes allow them to see perfectly in the dark. During the day it is clear: Close your eyes and rest from the strenuous hunt.
Eagles nest in dry areas. They inhabit savannas as well as semi-deserts.
Many nations have their flags adorned with an eagle's head. It is a symbol of power and victory.
When an eagle grabs prey on the ground with its powerful talons, it has no chance.
What a sweet little pet! Newly hatched eaglets have soft, plush feathers.
This American top hat is a national symbol of the United States of America - just like the bald eagle.
On the run, this otherwise lethargic animal is as fast as a cat. When threatened, the raccoon rolls onto its back and attacks its enemies with its sharp claws.
The ground squirrel feels best in the Kenyan savannah, where it goes on night hunts for termites and ants.
The groundhog has incredibly strong front claws that can reach through the thickest surface to the termites.
It's hard to believe that burrowing owls can reach up to 1.65 m. And the stronger ones weigh up to 70 kg.
If you want to try digging a burrow for a groundhog, you have to dig a 13 m long tunnel.
Donkeys can live up to 40 years. They are very loyal and easy-going companions, but they can also be stubborn.
Donkeys are an essential part of the life of nomads in the steppes of Mongolia.
Donkeys are skilled navigators on both rocky terrain and dry land, but in humid climates their hooves require special attention.
Some shepherds use donkeys to protect their flocks from attacks by lynxes, bears, and wolves.
Donkeys are used as draft animals because of their endurance. However, they are often very stubborn.
This soft-bodied predator is very adaptable, especially comfortable where there is plenty of shelter, prey and water. Meow!
In the tropical rainforest, these loners sneak up on their prey - and then strike with lightning speed.
As a typical stealth hunter, these big cats are surprisingly quiet despite their body structure.
Did you know that jaguar cubs are as big as a house cat after just 6 weeks and go hunting with their parents?
Jaguar skin was already very rare during the time of the Aztecs and Mayan kings often adorned themselves with it.
Wow, look closely: That's not an insect, it's a hummingbird! It can flap its wings up to 50 times per second and fly in place.
Where does the hummingbird come from? Among other things, from the Lesser Antilles! There are sweet flowers there, their nectar gives it the strength to flap its wings.
A hummingbird doesn't just need its talons to find food. It hovers like a helicopter among colorful flowers, sucking nectar from them with its long tongue.
This little creature especially likes colorful flowers, how cute! And if it sometimes swallows a small insect in them, it doesn't matter at all. It just eats it. Yummy!
Hummingbirds are the fastest vertebrates and can even fly backwards and sideways to reach food.
The nocturnal platypus spends most of its time in the water. Its eyes are closed, so how can it find its way around? On land, it hides in a burrow.
When scientists first brought platypuses to Europe, everyone thought it was a stuffed animal joke. A duck in front, a water rat in the back.
Despite having webbed feet, the platypus moves surprisingly quickly on land. Its gait resembles that of a lizard.
This little mess looks like a living fossil. The oldest finds of this species are 4.5 million years old, and the modern platypus is more than 100,000 years old.
The original inhabitants of Australia, the Aborigines, use boomerangs for hunting. It is unclear whether they have ever caught platypuses with them.
The wolf is known as a forest animal and feels most at home in the forest. Its pack finds protection and prey there.
The Central European wolf is one of the few wild animals that lives even in densely populated areas of Germany.
In the fairy tale "The Wolf and the Seven Little Goats", the wolf reveals himself with his black paw. Read it again quickly!
Children like plush wolves because they resemble domestic dogs.
Wolves appear in many fairy tales as villains or in horror films as werewolves.
The green iguana lives in trees, where it can blend in with its surroundings. But it also feels at home in the water!
The green iguana is one of the largest lizards in the Americas. It is also an excellent climber and swimmer.
The iguana is able to camouflage itself well from its enemies, but if necessary, it is ready to use its strong and sharp claw.
Did you know that an iguana can hold its breath underwater for up to 30 minutes?
The green iguana looks like a small dragon and thus inspires kite makers around the world.
Ravens are incredibly intelligent. Just look at how they hide their food. They make sure no one is looking and then they hide it. How clever!
Ravens have been kept in the Tower of London for centuries. Back then, it was a prison. Torture and executions were common, and ravens became a symbol of this dark age.
When searching for food, it alerts other ravens with a loud screech.
Ravens have mostly black, iridescent feathers and a relatively long lifespan.
Ravens are so smart: they can plan complex activities in advance. It wouldn't be surprising if they were also good at counting.
Anteaters are solitary creatures that roam forested areas during the day and rest underground at night.
This strange animal moves in savannas, forests and swamps in different parts of the world.
The anteater also uses its very sharp claws to reach termites and ants.
The anteater has a great sense of smell, which it needs to sniff out its favorite treat!
Even an anteater can't resist the sound of a pan flute. It's no wonder that it has poor hearing.
The tarantula is active at night. During the day it burrows into its cave. When hunting, it jumps on its prey and infects it with its venom.
The name tarantula comes from the Italian city of Taranto, where this giant spider was first described.
These eight-legged spiders catch insects not with a web, but by lying in wait for their prey and jumping on it.
Tarantulas have enlarged hind eyes that face straight ahead, allowing them to see very well.
Most spiders weave their own webs to catch prey. Tarantulas do not need weaving skills to make webs.
Llamas live in herds. They spit at each other to create order in the herd or to keep their distance.
In the Andes, the llama moves with its hooves very confidently, perfectly adapted to the mountainous region.
Llamas are ungulates that have "pads" on their hooves.
People in the Andes have used llamas as draft animals since ancient times. Their wool is soft and highly sought after.
If threatened, llamas will spit on the attacker. They are masters at spitting gum.
The mandrill lives in tropical rainforests and likes fruits, seeds, leaves, mushrooms and small vertebrates. It most often forages on the ground.
Mandrills live in the dense tropical forests of central Africa, such as Cameroon. These primates communicate with each other with loud cries and grunts, making them easy to spot by hunters.
The mandrill moves on all fours on the ground, but climbs trees to sleep. Its legs and claws are ideal for this movement.
Like other primates, mandrills groom each other. This grooming serves as both communication and as part of social behavior.
The body of the African drum called the djembe is made from the hollow of a tree trunk, which is covered with goatskin. The rhythm of these drums can be heard throughout Africa.
Mmm, fish! The seals have fresh fish every day. Sometimes some krill and they have a great dinner!
Seals prefer to roam around the Arctic Ocean and Antarctica. They have a thick layer of blubber that keeps them warm. They also like to sleep on floating ice floes.
Even though seals are like missiles in the water, they breed on land. They move forward with their powerful flippers.
A seal's mustache is essential for survival. It is necessary for the sense of movement in the water, including the movement of their prey. Without it, they would certainly starve.
Seals look quite harmless because of their large eyes, but their hunting instincts flare up around fish.
Ferrets are carnivores. They do not have a cecum, the part of the intestine that helps omnivores digest plants.
Even the ancient Romans used ferrets to combat rabbit infestations. Quite a large prey for such small mammals!
Ferrets have toe pads and cannot retract their claws. These pads on their front paws are a clue for trackers.
Ferrets are often kept as pets. They have been so domesticated that life in the wild would be very difficult for them.
What's that sound? A mouse! With this toy, your ferret will maintain its keen hunting instinct.
Puffins love fish and small crustaceans. Herring and sardines are very popular. Small puffins like to bite into snacks.

During mating, the colorful puffins frolic on the ground. They love the rocky cliffs of Ireland and the British Isles, but they also feel at home in Norway, Iceland, the Faroe Islands and the coast of the Brittany peninsula in France.
Because they spend their time both at sea and on land, their webbed toes are put to good use.
During mating season, these cute puffins stand on a cliff and call out to their partners, who respond to their calls while they wait in the breeding den.
These birds need a lot of fish for nutrition and for their offspring. The incubation period is only 36 days, so you need to start catching fish!
Arctic foxes hunt lemmings and other small rodents. Their sense of smell is so sensitive that they can detect prey even through a thick layer of snow.
Arctic foxes live in the polar regions of northern Europe, Greenland, and Alaska.
Arctic foxes live in the tundra and on ice. They can cross large areas of land and ice while searching for new habitat.
Newborn Arctic foxes are blind, deaf and very small. Their smooth dark brown fur quickly turns light.
Arctic foxes are white as snow. Their fur is 70% wool, which keeps them warm.
Squirrels eat very healthily. They like insects, worms, nuts, seeds, berries and mushrooms.
Squirrels are common throughout Europe and northern Asia. In winter, they grow a coat-like coat of fur.
Their long, rounded claws are needed for climbing and holding food.
Squirrels are especially known for their bushy tails. They are very fluffy and help them with their balance when climbing.
Three magic nuts can make many dreams come true. Who knows how many magic nuts the squirrels have already hidden?
The kinkajou loves juicy fruit and delicious fruit nectar. Few animals are as addicted to fruit as the kinkajou. It is not for nothing that this sweet tooth has earned the nickname honey bear.
These agile climbers are particularly comfortable in the treetops of the tropical rainforest. They are very shy and will make a quiet snort if startled while eating.
Finding a kinkajou's paw print is almost impossible because they jump from tree to tree and rarely come down to the ground voluntarily.
The kinkajou is a very adept tree climber, aided by its strong tail, which it uses to hang from branches while sucking nectar from flowers with its long tongue.
Although the kinkajou is a social animal, it also marks its territory using its scent.
Margays are members of the cat family. As carnivores, they hunt rodents, birds, and lizards. They also eat fruit and insects.
Margay lives in the forests of South America - from Mexico to Argentina.
The Margay has very flexible toes and especially wide paws, which are great for climbing. Climbing a tree upside down? No problem!
Their spotted fur is highly prized by hunters. Today, these cats are protected. Fortunately, they are also very shy and difficult to spot in the treetops.
As a day and night hunter, the margay has very good eyesight and can find its prey even in the dark.
They use their strong beaks to pick up fruit. A little protein - in the form of insects, spiders and small reptiles - is always welcome.
The subtropical and tropical rainforests of the Andes are teeming with toucans. The colorful toucans are very popular with tourists.
The toucan's claw resembles a woodpecker's claw, which has two in front and two in back, and can easily grab onto any tree.
The toucan's stunning beak has a special function; it can regulate its body temperature by changing its blood supply according to the ambient temperature.
Rainmakers are used in religious ceremonies in the Andes. The small stones make the sound of rain and are certainly more relaxing than the calls of toucans.
Storks like frogs, fish, tadpoles, lizards and small rodents. Their beaks are an important tool in finding food.
Storks like temperate climates. The original species comes from Germany and their nests can be found on tall poles on farms.
One claw points backwards and the other three forwards. Their webbed feet are usually stunted. Their long legs carry them particularly majestically in flight.
Most storks live in colonies, but some are solitary - and they are known for their lifelong partnerships. How romantic!
Many people are familiar with the white stork. According to a fairy tale, a stork pecked a pregnant woman in the leg, and she then waited in bed for the stork to bring her a baby.
Jackals hunt in groups or alone. They will eat anything that comes their way: insects, birds, eggs or small reptiles.
Jackals prowl the savannah, bush, or open pastures. They sometimes follow lions on their hunts, howling when they catch their prey.
Jackals mate for life. Male jackals perform a courtship dance for their mates.
Jackals live in caves or underground dens. Some jackals nibble each other's fur as a sign of affection.
The Egyptian god Anubis is depicted with the head of a jackal. Jackals were believed to guide souls to the afterlife.
Mmmm, termites! With his long tongue he pulls termites out of their holes.
Like many Australian mammals, the spotted anteater is a marsupial. After a 14-day gestation period, two to four young are born.
Thanks to their long claws, they can easily reach termites and even dig burrows. They protect themselves from the midday heat by going underground.

The spotted anteater is a marsupial without a pouch. The young attach themselves to the fur around their mothers' breasts. When they move to a new burrow, the mothers carry their young on their backs.
Its long tongue helps it find termites. Don't you think it's spinning around in its mouth like a licorice stick?
Wildebeests travel long distances in search of green pastures. They do not like dry grass.
Herds of wildebeest roam the open savannas of the Serengeti in Tanzania.
Wildebeests are even-toed ungulates, meaning they have an even number of toes.
Wildebeest live up to 20 years, unless they fall victim to their natural enemies.
The colorful cup contains a lot of water. Unfortunately, thirsty wildebeests are not good at holding it with their hooves.
The emu has a long neck, so it can pluck leaves from trees and branches. Yummy!
Many flightless birds are native to Australia, including the emu. The mild winter is an ideal breeding season.
The emu runs across the steppes on two legs. Due to its weight and underdeveloped chest muscles, it cannot fly.
Many species of emus are now extinct or endangered. A stuffed emu is a great way to keep them close to your heart.
An emu lays between 5 and 15 eggs. Do you think the chicks are as colorful as these eggs?
The falcon loves meat and feeds on small mammals, birds and even reptiles.
Falconry is considered a status symbol. In Germany, people who want to become falconers must have a special permit.
Falcons use their talons to catch and hold their prey. Small animals can die from the sheer force of their attack.
Falcons appear in many fairy tales and myths. They are symbols of courage and heroic deeds.
Falconers must always wear gloves, otherwise a falcon could injure them if it lands on their hand.
Centipedes and larvae are its main food. It uses its long, pointed beak not only for food but also to maintain its balance so that it does not fall.
The kiwi is native to New Zealand and has become a national symbol.
Did you know that kiwis have small claws on the ends of their wings? But they are hidden under their feathers. They have 4 toes on their feet, which allows them to move very quickly.
New Zealanders love their kiwis. The people of New Zealand even call themselves "kiwis".
Kiwis are nocturnal creatures and are therefore more heard than seen. At night, they can be heard up to 5 km away!
Although the mustang is very modest, it enjoys fresh grass. There is more than enough of it on the prairies, which is why the wild mustang population thrives so well here.

Mustangs were brought to America by Spanish conquistadors. They were mostly horses of Arabian and Berber origin that had run away from their masters. Thanks to their natural temperament, they were able to easily adapt to life in the wild.
Mustangs have small, hard hooves, a strong back, and a muscular rump. Native Americans used them for hunting, fighting, and as draft animals.
At between 140 and 150 centimeters tall at the withers, Mustangs are among the smaller horses, but due to their wild nature, they do not make ideal pets.
Mustangs are notorious for their fierce nature and high jumps. Riding a wild mustang is one of the favorite cowboy disciplines at rodeos - a very dangerous sport.
Otters are predators and hunt underwater. Fish, frogs, crayfish and other small invertebrates are on their diet.
Otters are not only native to Germany, but also all over the world. They have not settled in Australia and a few remote islands.
Otters are very fast in the water and on land. Their claws help them move; some people say it looks like they are crawling.
Young otters learn to swim from as early as 2 months old! Splashing, wading and diving are great fun for them.
They have a fast metabolism, which means they burn about 15-25% of their body weight every day. Eat it up!
Butterflies consume liquid food using a tube-shaped proboscis. They sip sap, honeydew, or juice from rotting fruit. Yummy!
The largest species of butterflies, the so-called swallowtails, live in Jamaica.
The butterfly's legs look dwarfed by its enormous wings. They are made up of chitinous plates arranged in rings.
Like other insects, butterflies have eyes that are made up of up to 6,000 individual smaller eyes. Look into my eyes, dear!
As caterpillars, butterflies have a huge appetite and eat anything green and tasty. Their body weight doubles with each molt.
Scottish Highland cattle love the green grass of the Scottish meadows. Their milk is especially good!
The melody of the bagpipes stretches across the entire landscape while a log, called a "caber", is thrown through the air. A truly interesting country!
Scottish weather can be a bit fickle and the ground slippery and muddy, but that's no match for the strong hooves of cattle.
Strong and obedient highland cattle have very low demands and females can give birth without human assistance.
Tourists in Scotland often ask what Scots wear under their kilts. Do you know?
Juicy, tasty earthworms and crunchy insects are always on the menu, but moles also enjoy small vertebrates. Yummy!
Moles are unwelcome guests in gardens. They help loosen the soil, but their burrows are unsightly.
Their claws are ideally shaped for creating underground labyrinths and they can feast on worms and insects while they work.
Moles are not social creatures. Probably because they have poor eyesight. Their sense of smell and touch is particularly strong. They can even sense electricity.
Their shovel-shaped claws are ideal for digging. Perfect for strawberry gardens!
The dodo eats fermented fruit. Maybe he looks stupid because he is stupid?
The last dodo was spotted on the island of Mauritius. The animals that were brought to the island became predators, leading to its extinction.
The dodo lives mostly on the ground. It is quite large and very heavy in winter. It is a flightless bird and had no natural predators on Mauritius.
Sailors often ate its yellowish eggs. Unfortunately, the dodo was too trusting, which contributed to its extinction!
The Dodo only exists as a fossil. That will draw a lot of people to the zoo. Visitors will be amazed.
Carcasses don't last long in the African desert. Vultures circle until the large predators abandon their prey. Then they pounce on the entrails!
Vultures live in steppes, semi-deserts, and mountains. They can be found in Europe, Asia, and throughout Africa.
Vultures feed on the entrails of carcasses, but they prefer soft entrails. Their beaks and talons are ideal for obtaining food.
Vultures are very social and live in groups. They feel at home when they snuggle up together.
Vulture feathers look beautiful in a headband. What do you think? Isn't it pretty?
The bearded dragon eats anything that comes its way. Small vertebrates are a welcome snack!
These lizards can be found on the Australian continent. They reach up to 60 cm and live across most of Australia.
The bearded dragon likes to relax on tree trunks, poles, and stumps. It loves the sun and is a good climber.
The bearded dragon buries its eggs in wet sand and does not guard them. Sometimes up to 35 young can hatch - that's a lot of brothers and sisters!
In addition to hunting small vertebrates and invertebrates, they also like fresh apples or other fruits.
Ants and termites are its favorite food. Its tongue is coated with sticky saliva, which helps it catch insects.
Pangolins are found in forests and savannas from Africa to Asia.
A pangolin's claw is like a multi-functional tool! Ideal for digging holes and climbing trees.
Baby pangolins have very soft scales. However, these harden over time. When the pangolins are still young, they can ride on their mother's tail - it's very comfortable!
Pangolin earrings are very cute. They look like exotic pine cones hanging from the ears.
Mouflon herds roam in forests or on cliffs. They love fresh grass.
European mouflons like to live in France. The environment there, with its abundance of good feed, suits them.
If mouflon cubs are threatened by wolves, their mothers will lead them to rockier areas where the wolves will not follow them.
The young also have to watch out for eagles. The forests will give them sufficient protection.
French cheese specialties are a world delicacy. Try the mouflon cheese with rosemary. It's a revelation with the scent of mountain herbs.
As a breeding breed, these pigs are used to living around people. They fatten up on fruits and vegetables, which keeps them nice and warm.
The Mangalica breed was once very popular in Hungary. The woolly pigs were able to withstand difficult living conditions.
Like other pig breeds, mangalicas love mud and wallow in it even in winter.
Mangalica cubs are striped like wild boars. The wavy coat appears later.
Have you ever tried to comb such a wild mane? The wool is quite coarse, but it hides a warm undertone.
Like other dogs, the Puli loves meat. It is used to living with people and was domesticated centuries ago.
The Puli dog is very popular in Hungary. Hungarians show their love for this dog by saying: That's not a dog, that's a Puli!
Puli love to run and are very active. They are very warm in the summer due to their thick coat, but in the winter they like to frolic and frolic.
Once puppies lose their fur, it starts to grow back thicker. The fur gradually grows and becomes fluffier, eventually looking like a thick sweater!
Thanks to his strong natural instincts, he is an ideal herding dog. Sometimes his vision can be impaired when his fur gets in his eyes, but there is one solution for that: simply tie his fur!
Komodo dragons use their tongues to track down prey. As active hunters, they will either dig up small rodents or pounce on ground-dwelling birds. They also occasionally enjoy bird eggs.

Komodo dragons live in Indonesia and the Lesser Sunda Islands, such as Komodo Island. They can be found in a variety of habitats, including savannas, grasslands, monsoon forests, and mangrove forests.
The Komodo dragon has claws that are adapted to any environment. As a cub, it likes to climb trees and as an adult, it searches for prey on the ground.
A young Komodo dragon hatches in a clutch of about twenty leathery eggs. The eggs weigh up to 125 grams.
The poison in the Komodo dragon's glands helps it hunt. It is the only way it can catch a larger animal. If it doesn't kill it right away, it will surely die of blood poisoning a few days later.
The long-tailed skunk loves the larvae of the beetles. It does not disdain fruit, nuts or seeds. It uses its long fingers to test unripe fruits and extract the ripe flesh.
Several species of the genus Ksukola - including the large Ksukola - are now extinct. The remaining species live mainly in the forested areas of Madagascar.

Its long spider-like fingers are one of its most distinctive features. Some cultures see this animal as a good omen, others interpret it as a sick spirit. They are also often attributed with magical powers.

The tailed foxes have several types of communication sounds. They call to each other with a "eeep" sound, and the cubs call to their mother with a distinctive "scree" sound. When cuddling, they mumble "gnofff" to each other, but when attacking, they scream "aaaak". When it's time to escape, they release with a "hay-hay" sound. Good, right?
When a red-tailed hawk drinks, it quickly moves its long third toe back and forth, splashing water into its mouth. It might use a straw!

Like all wolves, the werewolf is a predator. Its prey are other animals, including humans. According to legend, after being bitten by a werewolf, the victim also turns into a werewolf. A terrible thing!

Myths about wolfmen and werewolves were already present in ancient Greece. For example, in Ovid's Metamorphoses, Zeus transformed a man into a wolf who served human flesh at a feast. That's horrible! Absolutely unimaginable!

We've all seen scenes from horror movies, right? You know the one where seemingly peaceful people suddenly turn into terrifying werewolves? It starts with the hands - hair appears and the nails become claws... aaaaah!
Get one of these cuddly friends! Whoever is brave enough to take him to bed can handle even a real werewolf attack!
Do you hear that eerie howl in the dark night? Those are werewolves howling at the moon because of their miserable existence! Do you get goosebumps too?

A fresh insect salad as an appetizer, a small vertebrate as a main course, and a juicy snail for dessert. Scorpions love meat! And thanks to their dexterous and strong claws, they can often indulge in it.
The largest species of scorpions live in the desert regions of Mexico. They hide from the sun under rocks or in the sand during the day and go hunting at night.

Scorpions are a member of the order Arachnids and, like other members of this class, are characterized by the fact that one of their pairs of limbs has evolved into a tactile organ, the pedipalps. In the case of scorpions, these pedipalps are also topped with powerful claws, which, in addition to their tactile function, also serve as weapons.
Scorpion mating is preceded by an elegant courtship. The pair clasp their claws together and dance across the desert. After birth, the young scorpions cling tightly to their mother's back.

Once a scorpion spots its prey, it has two weapons at its disposal to catch it: powerful pincers and a nimble stinger. Warning: Some species of scorpion are among the most venomous creatures in the world.
Little quokkas can climb up to 1.5 meters to find juicy leaves. In times of need, they will also eat grass and plants.
Cute quokkas hop around in southwestern Australia and also live on Bald and Rottnest Islands.
If they want to increase their speed, they hop on their hind legs. Unlike kangaroos, they do not use their tail for balance.
Like other marsupials, females carry their young in their pouches for up to 28 days.
Rottnest Island is also nicknamed Rat Island because of its tiny inhabitants. A Dutch navigator mistook quokkas for rats and so named the island. Perhaps it was just a disguise?
Turkeys have a varied diet. Young birds love insects, but later they will also enjoy seeds, berries, nuts and roots. Turkeys swallow small stones to clean their stomachs and aid in digestion.
Many Native American tribes included turkey as part of their diet, and in modern times, turkeys are a staple of Thanksgiving dinner.
Strong claws are needed especially in winter, when they have to search for and dig for food.
Fleshly growths hang from its neck. They can be up to 8 cm long.
In North America, turkeys are a traditional part of the Thanksgiving dinner, and this harvest tradition has spread to Europe.
Marmot loves grass and herbs. But as a typical rodent, he also likes fruits, seeds and insects.
Marmots don't like the heat and prefer to live in the subarctic regions of Canada and the northern United States. They like cool climates because their fur is too warm.
Groundhogs dig extensive underground tunnel systems with escape routes and dead ends that can be used as toilets. They're smart, aren't they?
Marmots are deep sleepers. Their hibernation period can last up to 9 months. They curl up in the grass in their sleeping chambers.
Observers at the Groundhog Day wait for the groundhog to emerge from its burrow. If the groundhog casts a shadow, it means that there will be another 6 weeks of cold weather.
Weasels are predators - from the same genus as martens, but the young are very cute. Weasels are especially suitable for hunting in mouse tunnels.
The common weasel is found far and wide. It loves cold regions, but can also be found in Mediterranean climates and North Africa. In Eurasia, it is popular as a pest hunter.
When running, crawling, sneaking or digging... the weasel's claws are versatile. However, when hunting, it uses its teeth and bites its prey in the throat.
Weasel babies weigh about 1.5 grams at birth. They are blind at birth and on average about 5 babies are born. It takes about a month for them to open their eyes.
Weasels have perfect camouflage! At least they don't need a can of paint. Their fur automatically turns white in winter. They blend in perfectly with the snowy surroundings.
Huskies love meat - just like other dogs! But because they live mainly with people, they eat whatever they can get their hands on.
The weather in Canada can be very cold. Its fur provides perfect protection from the elements. If it gets stuck in the snow, it will stick its nose under a branch and spend the night under the snow.
Huskies have small paws compared to other dogs, which helps reduce the risk of heat loss and injury.
Huskies have a great sense of direction. They won't get lost even if everything is covered in snow. That's why Eskimos love them so much.
Husky puppies are raised by Eskimos along with their own children. They are part of the family and are, of course, allowed to enter the igloos.
The Hoatzin can eat both toxic and poisonous plants. Thanks to digestive bacteria, it can completely process the poison. Its claws are very muscular and callused.
The Hoatzin feels at home in tropical rainforests. It loves living near rivers and lakes. Water is a very important part of its home.
Their wings are not well developed, so they don't fly very well. Instead, they have claws, which are very important for movement.
The young live in nests that are built over water. They can jump from the nest directly into the water. Whoop! Splash! They hide in the water from predators.
After jumping into the water, the young climb back up the tree. Their claws help them do this. Over time, they lose these claws.
Because they have no fur, Sphynx cats are very active, otherwise they will lose body temperature. For this reason, they need a lot of food.
This cat is considered sacred and is worshipped throughout Egypt. In ancient times there was a cult of this cat with many statues. They were originally bred in Canada.
Like any other cat, the Sphynx loves to scratch trees and soft pillows. She also likes to sneak around quietly!
If anyone thinks that a Sphynx cat has a higher body temperature than its fur, they are wrong. If you touch it, it is warm because it gives off a lot of heat through its skin.
Without a protective coat, Sphynx cats are defenseless against the sun's rays. Just like humans, they need to be protected from sunburn. How about sunscreen?
These hunters hunt for prey in the deserts of the Arabian Peninsula. They are not picky and eat everything from rodents and lizards to small birds and insects. They like everything!
The cinnamon-colored stripe on the desert fox's back gives it a special desert touch!
The pads on this fox's feet are perfectly equipped for wandering through the desert and climbing date palms.
The desert fox has between 2 and 6 young, but most do not survive. Once they leave the den, they find themselves in danger.
When a desert fox prowls the Sahara during the day, it can't do without a parasol. Fortunately for it, it's a nocturnal creature.
The manul is about the size of a domestic cat. It has thick fur that makes it look much larger. The manul hunts small prey such as pikas, mountain partridges and mice. Meow!
At night, the manul prowls around the rocky steppes of Afghanistan and can also be found in mountains up to 3,000 meters. The manul loves rocks!
It is not entirely certain whether the manul is closer to lynxes or cats. In any case, its velvet paws resemble our cute cats.

The manul - also known as the steppe cat - is on the verge of extinction. Its way of life is being studied through scratchings in caves. Their tracks have also been discovered at higher altitudes, proving that they live in higher mountain ranges than previously thought.
This nocturnal animal is very shy and sleeps in a cave or comfortable crevice during the day. At night, it hunts and stalks its prey like a real cat.

Their extremely long canines allow them to catch prey such as monkeys or birds in trees. But that's just an easy meal. On the ground, they can catch wild game such as deer or wild boar. They just crouch down, jump up and bite their necks.
The clouded leopard lives in the wild in parts of Asia, such as China. Due to hunting for their fur and deforestation, these leopards are an endangered species.
Like many other cats, the clouded leopard can retract its claws and give them a rest. After all, they need them when climbing trees.
Clouded leopard cubs don't have to be afraid of heights! They are born high up in the trees. But they are protected by the hollows of the trees, where they are well hidden and don't fall so easily.
The clouded leopard is quite clumsy on the ground, but it really excels at climbing trees. It doesn't even need a climbing rope! It can even climb trees headfirst! It's a true artist in motion!
Yummy! Fruitgliders love to suck sap from trees. They have special teeth that make holes in the bark. But they won't refuse fruit, insects or small vertebrates!
These cute marmosets are found mainly in the Amazon in northern Brazil. They love forests, whether rainforest or tropical. As long as there are trees!
heir fingers and toes have claws instead of nails, which help them with vertical climbing on trees. In the treetops, they have no competition!
The young are usually born as fraternal twins. They only stick around their mother during nursing and are then seen around their father and with other members of the monkey group.
Oh no! It looks like the little marmosets fell into the paint. Or maybe they painted their ears with a paintbrush? Marmosets are cunning!
Their slightly curved beak is ideal for searching for tasty crabs and small worms.
This exotic bird lives in Brazil, the Amazon estuary, and throughout northern South America. It is even the national bird of the island of Trinidad in the Lesser Antilles.
The scarlet ibis breeds in the spring on mangrove islands and builds its nests in bushes and trees. It's a good thing they can hold on to trees with their talons!
Red ibis chicks have brown feathers at birth. The typical red coloration is achieved at two years. At puberty this is practical, as they reach adulthood at three years!
This slender bird looks as if someone has painted it with lipstick. Its beautiful plumage makes it a star among exotic birds!
Like other dogs, pugs love meat. However, pugs need a well-balanced diet because they gain weight quickly.

The pug was bred more than a thousand years ago in China. However, the royal patronage of the breed comes from Great Britain. Many artists have created portraits of their Pugs. After all, they are still considered fashionable dogs.
The pug stands out not only for its small and cute paws, but also for its round head and bulging eyes. The short muzzle often causes breathing problems in overbred animals.
This creature must be loved by everyone. The Pug was originally a highly revered dog. Only the Chinese emperor had the privilege of touching it.
The art of breeding was perfected in China about 2000 years ago. Since then, the pug has been known as an artistically bred breed. Many influential artists have made portraits of pugs.
The cheetah tracks its prey, runs towards it and catches it between its legs. Small antelopes and impala fall easily and are easy prey.
Cheetahs live mostly in savannah and steppe. They love tall grass and open areas for observation. Trees and bushes are not their favorites because they get in their way when they sprint.
Cheetah paws have strong and scaly pads, which are very useful during sprints.
After birth, cheetah cubs have long, silvery hairs on their backs, which fall out after three months. It is also said that these help to hide the cubs.
Cheetahs are the fastest animals in the world, running up to 93 km/h.
These cheerful primates enjoy leaves, fruit and sometimes flowers. They prefer to eat food in groups - it tastes better that way.
The proboscis monkey lives on the coast of Borneo, where there are mangrove forests and a lot of rain. It often jumps from trees straight into the water. Splash!

The proboscis monkey stays near the coast and is usually active during the day. It is only a bit sleepy in the morning and early afternoon. There is nothing better than a jump into the cold water to wake it up!
Unlike males, females have small nostrils. But even the big, thick nose of our long-nosed kahaua is pretty, isn't it?
The proboscis monkey is an excellent swimmer - the best of all monkeys. And he can dive! Would his nose fit into a diving mask?
Like other breeds of sheep, Blacknose sheep like to graze on grass. These sheep are very docile and never stray. They also produce a lot of milk and wool.
The Wallis Black-nosed Sheep can't yodel, but they can climb well! If one gets lost, grab your shepherd's staff and bring it back!
The Valais black-nosed sheep live between mountains and valleys. They are perfectly equipped for mountain wandering and occasionally enjoy the alpine edelweiss.

They really do have wool everywhere! The Wallis Blacknose sheep is covered in wool from head to toe. Sometimes the wool even gets in their eyes. It might be best to tie it up in a ponytail when climbing mountains.
Hop, hop, hop! Lambs happily hop across the rocky countryside and adult sheep love the mountains. They have a strong natural character and wool that protects them from all weathers.

Dik-diks prefer leaves and grass. They will sometimes enjoy fruits, flowers, and pods. They usually do not require much water because they are very small. However, if water is available, they will occasionally drink a little.
The ideal place to live is dry steppes covered in shrubs, such as those in Tanzania.

Forward! These small antelopes are good at moving on their feet and are almost always on the run. They run zigzagging to hide behind bushes and warn other animals of predators with "dsik-dsik" sounds.
Dik-diks are so adorable. Even the males, who have small horns that tilt slightly backwards, are not scary at all.
Little dik-diks are agile, nimble and of course very cautious. They drink dew drops from the grass and have cute little snouts. They are miniature versions of large antelopes!
Geckos like to eat arthropods like spiders, insects, and snakes. They will also snack on overripe fruit, nectar, and pollen. Yummy!
The colorful geckos live in the rainforests and banana plantations of eastern Madagascar. Their habitat is threatened by hotels and insecticides.
The colorful Madagascar felsuma has sticky feet, which means it can run even on very smooth surfaces like glass.
The color and pattern of geckos change as they mature. When threatened, they simply remove their tail and twitch to confuse their enemies.
Geckos like to lie in the sun on the roofs of huts and houses. They need a lot of light and like high humidity.
Capybaras like to fill their bellies with fresh grass from the land. They'll occasionally eat a water plant or two. And because they're so big, they have to eat a lot.
You'll find these cheerful creatures in South America near swamps, ponds, and mangrove forests!
The capybara is able to run quite fast, but when it runs, it usually jumps into the water because it can swim even better.
Capybaras communicate with barking sounds, purring, or screeching when they are afraid, and chirping and grunting when they are happy.
They hide in bushes and thickets before the hot day. Capybaras are active at dusk.
The spectacled bear will eat anything it can get its hands on, but it prefers fruits from cacti and bromeliads. From time to time, it will indulge in fresh shoots or insects.
The spectacled bear lives in the tropical Andes, where it has plenty of space to live. It likes to romp in the humid rainforest, which offers it a rich supply of food.
Like many other bears, the spectacled bear is plantigrade, with short claws and longer front legs, which are ideal for climbing.
Mother and father bears care for their cubs in a den. Depending on food availability and seasonal conditions, up to four cubs may be born.
The spectacled bear gets its name from the white markings on its face: Some look like they're wearing glasses, while others have completely white noses. Very stylish!
Bandicoots have a mixed diet that includes insects and small animals. They don't even need any water. They get everything they need from their food!
When it gets dark in the Australian outback, bandicoots come out to play, only to return to their burrows before dawn.
Bandicoots have very strong claws that they use to dig their burrows. They can disappear under sandy soil in a matter of minutes!
Zoo visitors are sure to love these cuddly bandicoots!
Get out your shovel and dig a big hole!
These birds prefer tasty fruits, insects, mushrooms or eggs. Yummy!
From Aborigines to didgeridoos - in Queensland, Australia, tourists are not only treated to tongue twisters, but also to amazing sights.
The southern cassowary looks harmless, but its claws can cut through flesh like butter.
Cassowaries are monogamous, and males build nests of grass and leaves on the ground.
This bird is stubborn, literally - its helmet perfectly protects it from impacts.
The maned dog hunts small rodents using its long legs. Armadillos and birds are also part of its diet. Together with fruit, it really enjoys such a meal!
The maned dog prefers to roam in grasslands, scrubland, and wet areas such as swamps.
The maned dog sneaks up on its tiptoes after its prey. Insects, birds, and rodents have no chance of escaping!
Usually three cubs are born. The male helps with the raising, but then they go their separate ways.
The maned dog's mane is reddish-brown and very distinctive. The tip of the tail, the inside of the ears and the neck are white. Brush him thoroughly!
The anaconda can eat a lot of food thanks to its elastic skin and ability to relax its jaw. Enjoy your meal!
Anacondas love warm, humid places, which is why they thrive in the tropical rainforests of South America.
Like smaller snakes, anacondas crawl on land and in water by sliding their long bodies.
Zoo visitors have a unique opportunity to hug an anaconda - not the other way around!
Every once in a while, the anaconda sheds its skin and gets a new, modern look!
The rainforest is full of uncontrollable growth, huge trees, climbing plants and wild animals. Sometimes even completely overgrown ruins are found. How exciting!
Liana is not a separate genus of plants, but a climbing plant of various plant species. It is also a popular means of transportation for monkeys and Tarzan.
Right, left, up, down - the jungle can be very dense and it's not easy to find your way around. So don't forget your compass!
Even in daylight, it can be dark in the jungle. We have an oil lamp for those occasions!
The jungle without insect repellent can be very unpleasant. Can a fly swatter really help us?
Great rocky landscapes repeatedly inspire our imagination: How did these rocks get here? Usually by erosion or glacial movement, but sometimes it's stories and legends.
Cliffs or steep, rocky slopes are popular climbing terrains for all adventurers.
A skilled sculptor can carve the most beautiful works from large boulders.
Jagged rocks and difficult terrain provide sufficient protection for birds and are also safe for nesting.
Stonehenge is one of the oldest examples of stone construction. Many mysteries, legends and stories surround this circle of standing stones.

Contrary to expectations, there are many species of animals that crawl across the hostile environment of the wasteland. The soil is not fertile, but it provides a haven for animals that have adapted well there.
The lights are only a few centimeters long and only light up for a few seconds.
Thriving landscapes are very often completely destroyed by modern technologies.
A single drop of oil can contaminate 600 liters of water.
Salinity is a natural cause of desert development.
The savanna is located between tropical rainforests and deserts. The term means something like "distant plain".
Fires play an important role in the savanna ecosystem: parts of dead plants are removed and nutrients are returned through a natural process known as remineralization.
The most famous savanna is in Africa and is called Serengeti.
By far the most common savanna herbivores belong to the insect family. Ants and termites clean the savanna.
The baobab can store a large amount of water. Who would have thought that the trunk can measure up to 20 meters in circumference!
Do your animals have sweet tooths too? They'll love Sugar Land! Don't forget to brush their teeth!
Cocoa butter melts quickly in your mouth. Chocolate has been around as a delicious snack since around 1500 BC.
Baked, toasted or just plain - this soft chewy candy is a big hit, especially in the US. In the past, it was made from plant products and was considered a medicine!
Sugar Land - a land full of milk, honey, and flowing chocolate! You'll also find fruit here - your animals' favorite snack.
What's flying around here? A muffin? A delicious treat for your animals - it falls straight into their mouths!
The tundra is permanently covered in frost, so-called permafrost (e.g. in polar regions), so don't forget to dress appropriately.
Peat is composed of rotted plant remains. It occurs in swamps and is often used as fuel.
The tundra has soft swamps and is also covered in hard permafrost. A good shovel for every type of soil is a definite must.
Inuit people often live in the tundra. They are easily recognized by their warm, furry hoods.
Mosses, lichens, and shrubs grow in the tundra. These plants are very hardy and can survive in any environment.
Searing heat, changing mirages and distant oases: The desert has always served as a source of inspiration, and not only for the stories from the collection One Thousand and One Nights!
There are many different types of dunes, such as shifting sand dunes. The longest sand dune is Abu Muharek, which is approximately 600 kilometers long.
Rain showers are rare in the desert, but when it does rain, it really pours!
Deserts, are they rare phenomena? Really? All deserts together may cover one-fifth of the Earth's surface.
Thanks to their ability to retain water, cacti can survive very long periods of drought.
What's so great about a terrarium? It allows you to raise exotic animals in their own habitat. Aren't these miniature worlds absolutely fascinating?
Exotic environments need a constant supply of air. Most terrariums require a humid and warm climate.
Reptiles and other small animals like to retreat to secluded caves.
The interior and exterior glass panels of terrariums must be cleaned regularly so that zoo visitors can see what is inside.
Are animals homesick? This can't happen! Set up the right photo backdrop and they'll immediately feel like they're in their natural habitat.
Hooray for the mountain meadow! High in the mountains, your animals will love it. Quickly call them and let's go!
The Alpine horn functions like a telephone in mountain pastures. It can be heard five to ten kilometers away.
There are no fences in the mountain pastures. Instead, cowbells are heard to alert farmers where their cows are grazing.
Under the snow? No way! Snowboarding is a quick way to get to the valley when there's snow everywhere.
The terrain in the mountains is very uneven. The pure nature is irreplaceable. A walking stick will help with stability on steep slopes.
It's hard to believe - visitors' eyes pop out of their sockets. Look what the cleaner Slavek is doing - visitors feel the gaze on their backs.
The first year has passed and ZooMumba has almost 10 million zoo directors.
After 12 months, there are over 65 animal species in ZooMumba! Do you have them all in your zoo?
At first, the animals at ZooMumba could only eat gummy bears and licorice snakes. But then enough was enough. The animals wanted something real to eat!
The story of Zoomumba began in August 2010. How time flies!
It's hard to believe - visitors' eyes pop out of their sockets. Look what the cleaner Slavek is doing - visitors feel the gaze on their backs.
At the end of the fourth year, ZooMumba has over 16 million zoo directors.
Four years and hundreds of different animals... Do you have them all in your zoo?
At first, the animals at ZooMumba could only eat gummy bears and licorice snakes. But then enough was enough. The animals wanted something real to eat!
The story of Zoomumba began in August 2010. How time flies!
Many animals build dens to protect themselves from enemies. This type of housing offers safety and keeps them warm.
It's not just animals that seek out caves to protect themselves from predators. Cavemen did the same! Caves hide many archaeological treasures.
The constant dripping of water leaves behind small calcium deposits that form stalactites.
The deeper the cave, the darker it gets. Bats don't need eyes to find their way, they use sonar instead.
Most caves are located in rock formations, and since rocks are constantly falling, it is wise to wear a helmet. Not only does it offer protection, but it also has a light attached to it.
Forests are home to a variety of trees, shrubs and bushes. The undergrowth provides shelter for many shy animals.
The Scandinavian forests are home to several other forest dwellers. Trolls are also spotted here and there!
There are many things to discover in the forest. It's easy to get lost, which is why markers are necessary!

In spring and summer the forest is lush with vegetation and green leaves, but in winter the soil is somewhat barren. The meadow with fresh hay is a welcome source of food for the animals during the winter season.
Forests are important suppliers of wood. Many small forest creatures seek refuge among the piles of logs.
Hurray for the water! The sound of the sea can be heard in the distance between the beach and the sand dunes. Millions of microorganisms live in the muddy banks of the intertidal zone.
Rough coastlines can be very dangerous for ships. Lighthouses help them navigate. In the past, pirates took advantage of this by lighting false signals.
What makes your heart beat faster than being shipwrecked? The sight of land, of course! The shore has saved the lives of many shipwrecked people.
No one knows when or where a bottle will appear. Some wash up right on the shore, while others sail around the globe. How exciting it is when the news finally arrives!
Buoys mark the boundary between the bay and maritime traffic.
When mushrooms sprout, it means the soil is very fertile. Who knows what surprises await us!
In the fairy forest, mushrooms are inhabited by little dwarves.

Although a mushroom has its roots in one place, it is considered part of the animal kingdom rather than the plant kingdom. It lives on organic nutrients and would definitely like to go for a walk. Why not give it a try?
Mushroom pickers must be careful when picking mushrooms, as many are poisonous. Pick only the good ones and put them in your basket!
Mold is actually a fungus, too. Like many of its counterparts, it prefers to grow on spoiled food.
The white-cheeked owl prefers to feast on moths, crickets, or small rodents. It searches for these delicacies from above and catches them in flight.
The white-cheeked shrike is native to Africa and also Cameroon. It lives in savannas, tropical moist forests or on the edges of forests.
The white-cheeked owl has transversely serrated claws partially covered with feathers.
Before mating, the male calls a two-syllable "hoo-hoo." The contact call sounds like "twee-twee," and the young beg for food with a hiss. Cute, right?
Whoa! Who's that grinning in the bushes? A white-faced owl can give a real scare in the dark!
These little monkeys spend half the day hunting insects, which they enjoy along with fruit.
These monkeys love forested areas. They swing from tree to tree and are mainly found in the Amazon and Brazil.
The squirrel monkey has very short fingers and its thumbs do not have the ability to oppose the other fingers like a human's.
The squirrel monkey lives in groups in perfect harmony. The young are often born in one group and at the same time.
These monkeys have a distinctive skull-like pattern on their faces. They don't have a striking hairstyle, but they do have prominent ears. Very funny!
The savannah pig is a herbivore. It prefers to feast on grasses, tubers, and roots.
The savannah pig lives in the forested landscapes and savannas of Africa, south of the Sahara.
When grazing, it crawls on all fours and digs into the ground, looking for all sorts of treats.
The savannah pig was nicknamed "Radio Africa" ​​because it often raises its tail like an antenna before attacking or escaping.
Did you know that the meerkat's favorite playmate is the savannah pig? They enjoy feasting on cockroaches, beetles, grubs, and worms. The only problem is that they make them a little bloated!
The Canada lynx feeds mainly on small mammals, but its favorite prey is the snowshoe hare. The two populations interact with each other.
The Canada lynx loves forested and snowy areas. It feels at home in Canada, mainly because there are many snowshoe hares there.
Its hind legs are longer than its front legs, which makes it look quite funny. It also has a lot of hair on its paws, allowing it to walk on snow with ease.
The young ones hide under tree roots and in thickets of fallen wood. From their hiding places they have a good view of the surroundings.
The Canadian lynx tracks down its prey and often waits a long time before attacking. A comfortable pillow like this would really come in handy while it waits.

The kingfisher hunts its prey - fish, aquatic insects and tadpoles - by diving in flight. It dives headfirst into the water, its eyes open, and when it resurfaces, it soars like a rocket. Fascinating!
The kingfisher feels at home in northern Scandinavia, where there are many freshwater sources in which to hunt. However, as a migratory bird, it can also be found in many other areas.
The legs of this bird change color. Young birds have dark brown legs that turn orange-red during the breeding season.
The monogamous kingfisher has a special mating ritual: the male hands the female a small fish with a bow. It's cute!
When feeding their young in the nest, the kingfishers circle around the nest to get to everyone. Hopefully they don't get dizzy!
Thanks to its divided upper lip, the wombat selects grass, plants, moss, roots and mushrooms during the long evening hours.
Like many other Australian animals, the wombat looks funny. It is a marsupial and lives in southern and eastern Australia.
With their sharp claws, they build intricate underground tunnels with comfortable dens. Who knows, maybe they even have furniture there?
The female's pouch faces backwards, preventing sand from getting into it when digging. The young remain in the pouch for up to eight weeks, after which they live near their mother for a year.
If an enemy approaches the burrow, the wombat will simply close the entrance with its back. It serves as a shield and is quite well padded thanks to its thick skin, cartilage and bones.
Pelicans feed mainly on fish. They have a very sophisticated way of hunting: they fly from a great height and plunge their strong beak directly into the water.
Pelicans are found all over the world. Outside the breeding season, they roam New Guinea.
Pelicans are part of the order of copepods and waders. Their toes are connected by a webbed foot.
During mating, the male has a brightly colored face. He woos the female with a dance in which he stretches his head and inflates his esophageal sac.
Pelicans use their esophageal pouch as a fishing net.\n It can hold up to 13 liters of water and of course... lots of fish!
Peck, peck, peck. Like other chickens, silkies like to peck at grains. Their crested feathers shake nicely.
According to ancient records, silk chickens, with snow-white feathers and black skin, were first seen in China.
The feathers of these chickens also cover their legs, which is why they look like fluffy, furry balls.
Silky hens are good foster mothers. They lay up to 80 eggs per year and are very reliable.
During Easter, these hens would appreciate it if people ate carrots instead of their eggs so they could have more chicks!
Juicy grass, birch and willow leaves, lichens and mosses - this is a delicacy that musk deer certainly cannot resist.
The musk ox feels at home in the tundra, where there is not much precipitation. And when everything starts to melt, it also finds lush vegetation there. Great!
With its wide, rounded, and sharp hooves, it stomps across the tundra, scraping away snow and crushing ice.
The young are able to forage just one week after birth, but they continue to feed on their mothers.
During mating season, musk oxen release a substance into their urine that smells sweet, which is where the scent gets its name and becomes a kind of trademark.
Dingoes prey on almost any mammal that walks on its feet, has fluffy fur or pouches. Most Australian mammals are marsupials, so you should be wary of dingos.
Dingoes were once quite rare and lived close to the Australian Aborigines. Today, they can be found all over Australia.
Their legs are very long, with the hind feet making up a third of their hind limbs. They lack a fifth claw entirely.
Dingoes live in groups and are very social, although they are mostly seen alone.
Dingos are related to domestic dogs and are called wild dogs in Australia.
It can strike its victim with its strong tentacles in 0.1 second.
Praying mantises live mainly in tropical and subtropical regions, where there are lots of insects. Yummy!
The front two legs are toothed and have pointed claws.
It's not uncommon for a much larger female to eat her partner after mating. Oh my!
Most praying mantises are diurnal predators that sit for hours waiting for their food. They are difficult to spot in the grass.
If a macaque is dripping fruit juice from its whiskers, it means it has just enjoyed a juicy fruit. In addition to fruit, it loves leaves, buds, insects and sometimes even small vertebrates. Groats!
There are only 2,500 adult macaques left in various parts of India. Help save them from extinction!
These good climbers spend most of their time high up in the treetops looking down!
Macaques live in groups of ten to twenty animals and have a strong hierarchy. They communicate with each other by screaming.
The striking lion's mane is their trademark. The tail is also very distinctive: the black tassel at the end of the tail looks like a lion's, which is why this monkey is called the "lion" macaque.
The ferret is an omnivore. It eats squirrels, small rodents, birds, but also berries and fruit. It can be easily tamed, which is why it is kept for hunting mice.
The ferret cat is found in many American states, but it prefers to live in dry forests and deserts with minimal vegetation and rocks.
This raccoon sneaks around at night looking for food. The North American ferret cat has retractable claws.
The ferret cat has large, purple-colored eyes, surrounded by fine fur. It looks as if it is wearing glasses, which also makes its eyes appear larger.
The North American Indians called it "cacomistle", which means something like "half puma".
Spotted hyenas have very strong jaws that can crush a turtle's shell or even very strong bones, allowing them to utilize their food very efficiently.
Spotted hyenas inhabit rocky mountain areas, semi-deserts, and scrub savannas.
Spotted hyenas walk on their toes and their front legs are slightly longer than their hind legs, which is why they have a stooped back.
They usually have 4 to 5 young and about 30 days after birth they start playing together.
Hyenas not only hunt their own prey, but also feast on carrion, in other words, the remains of animals that have been torn to pieces by cheetahs, leopards, lions or tigers.
Marsh plants thrive here mainly thanks to the marshy soil and vegetation that always has enough water.
Small, playful lights twinkle all around, dancing merrily in the swamp. Are they ghosts or atmospheric gases in flames?
Archaeologists are searching the swamps for signs of ancient cultures.
In good conditions, creatures in swamps can be preserved for centuries.
The mud in the marshes is highly valued for its healing properties and is often used in therapies.
The oystercatcher's prey depends on the tides. As the water recedes, it picks clams, crabs, and insects from the bottom.
The oystercatcher is the national bird of the Faroe Islands and is strictly protected.
The oystercatcher not only searches for food in the sand, but also maneuvers quickly around rocks.
Oystercatchers live monogamous lives and can have young even at the age of 36.
The ocean waves constantly wash up new food for them. They force open the crab's shell with their beaks and sometimes swallow the mussels whole.
